summary: An academic book review of 'Socrates and the Socratic philosophies' that discusses scholarly perspectives on Socrates and his contemporaries. The text is largely about secondary interpretations and other Socratic philosophers, but it briefly outlines Socrates's use of reversal arguments (peritropē) and his foundational claim of knowing nothing.

principles.statement: I do not possess knowledge, except for the knowledge that I do not know anything.
principles.rationale: To distinguish true knowledge from mere belief, and to fulfill a divinely ordained mission to convince others that they also lack the knowledge they claim to have.
principles.source_quote: Socrates did not claim not to believe anything, just not to know anything (except that he knew that he didn’t know anything).

frameworks.name: Περιτροπή (Peritropē / Reversal Argument)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When refuting an interlocutor's claim during a philosophical or argumentative conversation.
steps: Demonstrate that the claim is already formulated in a way that entails its own contradictory.
frameworks.source_quote: refutes a speaker’s claim by showing that "it is already formulated so as to entail its own contradictory"

anti_patterns.description: Charging money for instruction, claiming to possess knowledge, and committing beliefs to writing.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These are the practices of the Sophists, who falsely claim to know things they do not actually know.
